lesson_title: Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.front: What is a C++ language standard?
cards.back: A language standard (like C++11, C++17, C++20) defines which features and syntax are available in C++. Newer standards add features while maintaining backward compatibility.
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.text: Major C++ standards include C++98, C++11, C++14, C++17, {{c1::C++20}}, and {{c2::C++23}}, released approximately every {{c3::3 years}}.
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.front: Which C++ standard should you use for new projects?
cards.back: Use C++20 or C++23 if your compiler supports it. These are modern standards with the best features. Avoid older standards unless required for compatibility.
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.concept: Why you should use modern C++ standards
cards.explanation:
Modern C++ (C++11 and later) is significantly better:
  • Safer features (smart pointers, nullptr, auto)
  • More expressive (lambdas, range-for, structured bindings)
  • Better performance (move semantics, constexpr)
  • Easier to write correct code
  • Industry best practices assume C++11 minimum
C++98/03 code is outdated and harder to maintain.
examples: C++11: auto x = 5; instead of int x = 5;
C++11: for (auto val : vec) instead of iterators
C++17: if (auto it = map.find(key); it != map.end())
C++20: concepts for better template errors
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.text: In Visual Studio, set the C++ language standard in Project Properties → C/C++ → Language → C++ Language Standard to {{c1::/std:c++20}} or {{c2::/std:c++latest}}.
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.front: What does /std:c++latest mean in Visual Studio?
cards.back: /std:c++latest enables all the latest C++ features that Visual Studio supports, including experimental features. Useful for trying cutting-edge features but less stable.
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.text: In GCC and Clang, set the C++ standard using {{c1::-std=c++20}} or {{c2::-std=c++23}} compiler flags.
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.front: Are newer C++ standards backward compatible with older standards?
cards.back: Mostly yes. C++ maintains strong backward compatibility, so C++98 code usually compiles in C++20. However, some deprecated features are eventually removed, and new keywords can conflict with old code.
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.front: Why might you need to check if a compiler fully supports a C++ standard?
cards.back: Compilers implement new standards incrementally. A compiler might claim C++20 support but not implement all features yet. Check compiler documentation for feature support.
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.question: Which C++ standard introduced the auto keyword for type deduction?
cards.code:
auto x = 5;      // compiler deduces type
auto y = 3.14;   // compiler deduces type
cards.explanation: The auto keyword for automatic type deduction was introduced in C++11, making it easier to write generic and concise code.
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.front: Should you set the C++ standard per-file or per-project?
cards.back: Per-project. All files in a project should use the same C++ standard for consistency. Mixing standards within a project can cause confusing errors.
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard

cards.concept: Choosing the right C++ standard for your project
cards.explanation:
Consider these factors when choosing a standard:
  • New projects: Use C++20 or C++23 for modern features
  • Compiler support: Verify your compiler supports the features you need
  • Team/company policy: Follow established standards
  • Library dependencies: Some libraries require specific standards
  • Maintenance: Don't use older standards unless absolutely necessary
Once chosen, document it and configure all projects consistently.
examples: Cross-platform: check compiler support on all platforms
cards.source: 0.12 - Configuring your compiler: Choosing a language standard
